**St. Ignace** ([/ˈɪɡnəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IG-nəss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in the U.S. state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Mackinac County](/wiki/Mackinac_County,_Michigan "Mackinac County, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-GR6-6) The city had a population of 2,306 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[4]](#cite_note-Census_2020-4) [St. Ignace Township](/wiki/St._Ignace_Township,_Michigan "St. Ignace Township, Michigan") is located just to the north of the city; the two are administered separately.

St. Ignace is located along [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron") in the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") on the northern side of the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac"). The city serves as a gateway to the state's Upper Peninsula for travelers coming from the [Lower Peninsula](/wiki/Lower_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Lower Peninsula of Michigan"), as the city is at the north end of the [Mackinac Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Bridge "Mackinac Bridge"), opposite [Mackinaw City](/wiki/Mackinaw_City,_Michigan "Mackinaw City, Michigan"). It has one of two ports which provide ferry service to nearby [Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island").

St. Ignace's history dates back to 1671 when French [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") priests founded the [St. Ignace Mission](/wiki/St._Ignace_Mission "St. Ignace Mission"), which makes it one of the oldest European settlements in the state after [Sault Ste. Marie](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan "Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan"). The area was previously inhabited by the [Wyandot people](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people"), as well as the [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe") and [Ottawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") tribes of Native Americans. St. Ignace soon became the center of [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") with the French. It was incorporated as a village in 1882 and a city in 1883. The [Mackinac Bands of Chippewa and Ottawa Indians](/wiki/Mackinac_Bands_of_Chippewa_and_Ottawa_Indians "Mackinac Bands of Chippewa and Ottawa Indians") is headquartered at St. Ignace, and the city continues to have a high population of Native Americans.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of Northern Michigan](/wiki/History_of_Northern_Michigan "History of Northern Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/2009-0618-StIgnace-MuseumMission.jpg/250px-2009-0618-StIgnace-MuseumMission.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009-0618-StIgnace-MuseumMission.jpg)

The Museum of Ojibwa Culture operates in the former St. Ignace Mission building. The mission is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). It was established at a historic [Wyandot](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people") (Huron) village.

Further information: [Jesuit missions in North America](/wiki/Jesuit_missions_in_North_America "Jesuit missions in North America")

St. Ignace is the second-oldest city founded by [Europeans](/wiki/Europe "Europe") in Michigan. Various cultures of [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") had inhabited the area for thousands of years before the first exploration here by French colonists. Early historic peoples of the area in the 17th century were predominantly the [Iroquoian](/wiki/Iroquoian "Iroquoian")-speaking *[Wendat](/wiki/Wyandot_language "Wyandot language")*, whom the French called the *[Huron](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people").*

By the early 18th century, the *[Anishinaabe](/wiki/Anishinaabe "Anishinaabe")* *[Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"),* who spoke one of the [Algonquian languages](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages"), became prominent in the region. Another related Anishinaabe people were the [Ottawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") or *Odawa* in their language. The third member of the [Council of Three Fires](/wiki/Council_of_Three_Fires "Council of Three Fires"), a loose confederacy of these tribes, was the [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") people. All three peoples have descendants who are members of various federally recognized tribes in northern Michigan.

French explorer and priest [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") founded the [St. Ignace Mission](/wiki/St._Ignace_Mission "St. Ignace Mission") on this site in 1671 and was buried here after his death.[[7]](#cite_note-sawyer-7) He named it for [St. Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/St._Ignatius_of_Loyola "St. Ignatius of Loyola"), founder of the [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") religious order, whose priests were active as missionaries across North America. (*Ignace* is the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") version of Ignatius.) Jesuits served at missions to convert [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada")/[Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") and to share French culture. In 1673, Marquette joined the expedition of [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet"), a French-Canadian explorer, and departed from St. Ignace on May 17, with two canoes and five [voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") of French-Indian ancestry ([Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis")) on a voyage to find the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). They were successful and descended downriver as far as Arkansas.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/2009-0618-StIgnace-JacquesMarquetteGrave.jpg/250px-2009-0618-StIgnace-JacquesMarquetteGrave.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009-0618-StIgnace-JacquesMarquetteGrave.jpg)

A monument marks the grave of Father Marquette, where he was buried next to the St. Ignace Mission, now used as the Museum of Ojibway Culture.

While separately exploring the Great Lakes region on the ship *[Le Griffon](/wiki/Le_Griffon "Le Griffon")* with [Louis Hennepin](/wiki/Louis_Hennepin "Louis Hennepin"), [Sieur de La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle") reached St. Ignace on August 27, 1679. [Louis de La Porte](/w/index.php?title=Louis_de_La_Porte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis de La Porte (page does not exist)"), Sieur de Louvigny founded [Fort de Buade](/wiki/Fort_de_Buade "Fort de Buade") here in 1681 as a [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trading "Fur trading") post. It was later directed by [Antoine Cadillac](/wiki/Antoine_Cadillac "Antoine Cadillac"). It was closed by the French in 1697.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Jesuits abandoned their mission in 1705.

The Ojibwe, who came to dominate most of the Native American territory of present-day Michigan in the 18th century, were allies of the French in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") against the British. After the British victory in the Seven Years' War, in 1763 they took over the territory of France in North America, including this part of the former [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). After the victory of rebellious colonists in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), in 1783 the village was included within the new United States, as part of what became called its [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory").

An important [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trading "Fur trading") site for both the French and the British, St. Ignace declined in importance by the early 19th century. The Ojibwe had allied with Great Britain in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), based on their long trading and a hope they would expel American colonists. The fur trade declined at St. Ignace largely because the United States prohibited British Canadian traders from operating across the border after the end of the war. At the same time European demand for North American furs was declining as tastes changed, and other parts of the economy grew.

Both British-Canadians and later Americans operated a larger trading center at [Sault Ste. Marie](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan "Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan"), which developed on both sides of the Canadian-US border, until the decline of the fur trade in the 1830s. The fur trade also suffered before and during the hostilities of the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), as the United States first imposed a boycott on all trade with England, including traders in Canada. Many local people kept businesses going by smuggling, but postwar prohibitions on the fur trade were more difficult to avoid. prohibited British traders from operating across the border, as had been their earlier practice. The Ojibwe had allied with the British, their longtime trading partners, during the War of 1812,

In 1882, construction of the [Detroit, Mackinac and Marquette Railroad](/wiki/Detroit,_Mackinac_and_Marquette_Railroad "Detroit, Mackinac and Marquette Railroad"), which connected the straits area to the major city of [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), provided an economic boost to the village. Farmers and the lumber industry could more easily get products to a major market. St. Ignace was incorporated as a village on February 23, 1882, and as a city in 1883.[[7]](#cite_note-sawyer-7)

In the late 19th century, a new sector of its economy developed, as it began to attract tourists as a popular summer resort and for its connection to [Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island").

Since the late 20th century, the city has become a rural destination for [heritage tourism](/wiki/Heritage_tourism "Heritage tourism") and is part of a regional area popular for summer tourism. A variety of water sports and activities are available.

The [Mackinac Bands of Chippewa and Ottawa Indians](/wiki/Mackinac_Bands_of_Chippewa_and_Ottawa_Indians "Mackinac Bands of Chippewa and Ottawa Indians"), a state-recognized tribe, is based in St. Ignace. It also has bands in several other counties in the region. With an enrolled membership of 4,000 in this area and state recognition, it has been seeking federal recognition since 1998. The larger federally recognized [Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie_Tribe_of_Chippewa_Indians "Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians"), based in the city of that name and the region of northern Michigan, owns and operates a gaming casino in St. Ignace on land it controls in the city, in addition to land and casinos in four other cities in the state.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 2.65 square miles (6.86 km2), of which 0.01 square miles (0.03 km2) are water.[[3]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2022-3) Point St. Ignace, which separates the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac") from [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron"), occupies the southern half of the city. The city is laid out along the west shore of Lake Huron, with the southern edge of the city facing the Straits. [Straits State Park](/wiki/Straits_State_Park "Straits State Park") is in the southern part of the city.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[10]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-10) of 2010, there were 2,452 people, 1,064 households, and 633 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 914.9 inhabitants per square mile (353.2/km2). There were 1,299 housing units at an average density of 484.7 per square mile (187.1/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 63.4% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 1.0% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 27.8% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.3% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.2% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 7.3% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 1.1% of the population.

There were 1,064 households, of which 27.0% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 43.3% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 11.1% had a female householder with no husband present, 5.1% had a male householder with no wife present, and 40.5% were non-families. 33.8% of all households were made up of individuals, and 13.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.24 and the average family size was 2.84.

The median age in the city was 44.5 years. 21.3% of residents were under the age of 18; 7.7% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 21.6% were from 25 to 44; 31.6% were from 45 to 64; and 17.7% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 48.2% male and 51.8% female.

## Education

[St. Ignace Area Schools](/wiki/St._Ignace_Area_Schools "St. Ignace Area Schools") is the local school district.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Local sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Local sights")]

[Kewadin Casino of St. Ignace](/wiki/Kewadin_Casinos "Kewadin Casinos"), is a significant tourist attraction and local employer. [Straits State Park](/wiki/Straits_State_Park "Straits State Park") is located at the south end of the city. [Wawatam Lighthouse](/wiki/Wawatam_Lighthouse "Wawatam Lighthouse") is located in the city's harbor. The harbor also is a port for Coast Guard [ice breakers](/wiki/Ice_breaker "Ice breaker"), *e.g.*, the tug *[Katmai Bay](/wiki/Katmai_Bay "Katmai Bay")*[[A]](#cite_note-14) and heavy duty breaker *[Mackinaw](/wiki/USCGC_Mackinaw_(WLBB-30) "USCGC Mackinaw (WLBB-30)")*.[[B]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-King-17)[[16]](#cite_note-USCG-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Heffernan-19)[[18]](#cite_note-Ellison-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-YouTube-22)

[St. Anthony's Rock](/wiki/St._Anthony%27s_Rock "St. Anthony's Rock"), a free-access geological [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") [stack](/wiki/Stack_(geology) "Stack (geology)"), is located in the center of the town. [Castle Rock](/wiki/Castle_Rock_(Michigan) "Castle Rock (Michigan)"), a similar but taller stack for which admission is charged, is located three miles (4.8 km) north overlooking [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron"). [Rabbit's Back](/wiki/Rabbit%27s_Back "Rabbit's Back"), a prominent promontory that also overlooks Lake Huron, is four miles (6.4 km) north. [Chain Lake](/wiki/Chain_Lake_(Michigan) "Chain Lake (Michigan)"), one mile (1.6 km) inland, features inland fishing opportunities.

Marie.

### Ferry services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ferry services")]

Two ferry companies ([Shepler's Ferry](/wiki/Shepler%27s_Ferry "Shepler's Ferry") and the [Arnold Transit Company](/wiki/Arnold_Transit_Company "Arnold Transit Company")) operate out of Saint Ignace, connecting tourists and commuters to [Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island").

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Airports")]

The nearest airports with scheduled passenger service are in [Chippewa County International Airport](/wiki/Chippewa_County_International_Airport "Chippewa County International Airport") in [Kinross](/wiki/Kinross,_Michigan "Kinross, Michigan") (northeast of St. Ignace, adjacent to I-75) and [Pellston Regional Airport](/wiki/Pellston_Regional_Airport "Pellston Regional Airport") in the [Lower Peninsula](/wiki/Lower_Peninsula "Lower Peninsula").[[21]](#cite_note-23)

### Bus service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Ignace,_Michigan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bus service")]

[Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails") provides daily intercity bus service between St. Ignace and [East Lansing, Michigan](/wiki/East_Lansing,_Michigan "East Lansing, Michigan"),[[22]](#cite_note-24) between St. Ignace and [Bay City, Michigan](/wiki/Bay_City,_Michigan "Bay City, Michigan"),[[23]](#cite_note-25) and between St. Ignace and [Ironwood, Michigan](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan").[[24]](#cite_note-26)
